title: Evolution — natural selection and adaptation
examTip: Natural selection questions on AQA papers almost always ask you to describe the mechanism as a series of steps. Learn the five-step process and practise applying it to unfamiliar examples — particularly antibiotic resistance, which is a favourite extended-writing question.
introTips: Natural selection is a mechanism, not a plan — organisms don't 'try' to evolve. It's a process of survival and reproduction, played out by chance variation over long periods of time.

The antibiotic resistance example is worth mastering in detail. It's observable in real time, it has huge public health implications, and AQA loves it as a context for testing whether you understand the mechanism properly.
Evidence for evolution often trips people up. Fossil record, antibiotic resistance, DNA comparisons — know what each one shows and why it supports Darwin rather than Lamarck.
intro: The diversity of life on Earth — millions of species, each fitted to its environment — didn't appear by design. It's the product of a simple process repeated over billions of years: individuals that happen to be better suited to their environment survive longer, reproduce more, and pass on the traits that helped them. Darwin called this natural selection. Understanding it doesn't just explain where species came from — it explains why bacteria are becoming resistant to our antibiotics right now, in hospitals across the world. This lesson covers the mechanism, the evidence, and the contrast with an earlier, incorrect theory.

keyPoints.heading: Natural selection — the five-step mechanism
keyPoints.content: Natural selection operates through a sequence of linked events. (1) Variation exists within a population — individuals differ in their characteristics due to different alleles. (2) There is competition for limited resources (food, mates, territory). (3) Some variants are better adapted — they survive and reproduce more successfully than others. (4) Those individuals pass on their advantageous alleles to offspring. (5) Over many generations, the frequency of the beneficial allele increases in the population, and the population gradually changes. This is evolution — the change in inherited characteristics of a population over time. The important point is that individuals do not evolve; populations evolve over generations.
writeBullets: 1. Variation exists in the population (genetic, due to different alleles)
2. Competition for resources — not all individuals survive
3. Better-adapted individuals survive and reproduce more (survival of the fittest)
5. Over generations, beneficial allele frequency increases — population changes
Individuals do not evolve — populations evolve

keyTerms.def: Change in the inherited characteristics of a population over successive generations
The process by which organisms with advantageous inherited traits survive and reproduce more successfully, increasing the frequency of those traits
An inherited feature that makes an organism better suited to its environment
How common a particular allele is in a population — natural selection changes allele frequencies over time

keyPoints.memTip: VCRAP: Variation → Competition → Reproductive success → Alleles passed on → Population changes. Five steps, one acronym. Run through them in order every time you describe natural selection.

keyPoints.heading: Antibiotic resistance — natural selection in action
keyPoints.content: Antibiotic resistance is not a historical process — it's happening right now and it's a direct consequence of natural selection. In a population of bacteria, random mutations occasionally produce an allele that makes an individual resistant to an antibiotic. If antibiotics are present, non-resistant bacteria are killed, but the resistant bacterium survives and reproduces rapidly. The resistant allele is passed to all offspring. Very quickly, the population is dominated by resistant bacteria. This process is accelerated by misuse of antibiotics (not completing courses, using them for viral infections) because more frequent exposure means more selection pressure. MRSA (methicillin-resistant Staphylococcus aureus) is the classic AQA example of a 'superbug' produced this way.
writeBullets: Mutation in one bacterium creates a resistant allele — by chance, not by need
Antibiotics kill non-resistant bacteria; resistant bacterium survives
Resistant bacterium reproduces rapidly — passes allele to all offspring
Population becomes resistant over many generations
Misuse of antibiotics (not completing courses) accelerates resistance
MRSA = classic 'superbug' example of antibiotic resistance

keyTerms.def: The ability of bacteria to survive exposure to antibiotics — an outcome of natural selection acting on chance mutations
Methicillin-resistant Staphylococcus aureus — a bacterium resistant to multiple antibiotics, a major hospital concern
An environmental factor (like antibiotics) that determines which variants survive and reproduce

keyPoints.memTip: Resistance isn't planned — the bacterium doesn't 'decide' to resist. A random mutation happened to be useful when antibiotics arrived. Remove the selection pressure (the antibiotic) and the resistant strain might not even be the most common. That's the key insight.

keyPoints.heading: Evidence for evolution and Darwin vs Lamarck
keyPoints.content: The fossil record shows gradual changes in organisms over millions of years — the older the rock, the more different fossils look from modern species. Antibiotic resistance demonstrates observable evolution in real time. Homologous structures — such as the similar bone arrangements in human arms, whale flippers, and bat wings — suggest descent from a common ancestor. DNA comparison between species shows that more closely related species have more similar DNA sequences. Darwin's theory of evolution by natural selection is now the accepted scientific explanation. Lamarck's earlier theory proposed that organisms could pass on characteristics acquired during their lifetime (e.g. a giraffe stretching its neck and passing on a longer neck to offspring) — this is now known to be incorrect because acquired characteristics are not encoded in DNA.
writeBullets: Fossil record: gradual change visible across geological time — older fossils less like modern species
Antibiotic resistance: evolution directly observable, within years
Homologous structures: similar bone arrangements in different species → common ancestor
DNA comparison: closely related species have more similar DNA
Darwin: variation + natural selection → gradual change over generations (correct)
Lamarck: acquired characteristics inherited (e.g. stretched neck) — incorrect, not encoded in DNA

keyTerms.def: The collection of fossils showing how life has changed over geological time — key evidence for evolution
Similar structures in different species that suggest descent from a common ancestor — e.g. pentadactyl limb in vertebrates
Early biologist who proposed that organisms pass on characteristics acquired in their lifetime — now known to be incorrect
Charles Darwin — proposed the theory of evolution by natural selection, supported by extensive evidence

keyPoints.memTip: FHDA evidence: Fossils, Homologous structures, DNA comparison, Antibiotic resistance (direct observation). Lamarck is the 'wrong' answer — acquired traits can't be inherited because they're not in the DNA.

tables.title: Darwin vs Lamarck — comparing theories of evolution
rows: Organisms acquire useful traits during life
Natural selection acts on existing variation
Giraffe stretches neck → longer neck inherited
Long-necked giraffes survived better → more offspring → allele spread
Disproven — acquired traits not encoded in DNA
Accepted theory — supported by extensive evidence

1.question: In natural selection, what happens to an advantageous allele over many generations?
options: It disappears because it gets used up by individuals who survive
Its frequency in the population increases as better-adapted individuals reproduce more
It is spread equally to all individuals in the population within one generation
It becomes dominant immediately, replacing all other alleles at once
1.correctFeedback: ✅ Correct — individuals with the advantageous allele survive and reproduce more, passing that allele to more offspring. Over many generations, the frequency of the allele increases in the population.
1.wrongFeedback: Natural selection increases the frequency of advantageous alleles over many generations. Better-adapted individuals survive and reproduce more → they pass the allele to more offspring → the allele becomes more common. This is a gradual process across generations, not an instant change.

2.question: A patient stops taking antibiotics halfway through the course because they feel better. Why could this accelerate antibiotic resistance?
options: The remaining bacteria mutate faster because the antibiotic is removed
Some bacteria may have survived with partial resistance — stopping early removes the selection pressure before they are eliminated, allowing resistant bacteria to multiply
The antibiotic becomes stronger when the course isn't completed
Bacteria share antibiotic resistance genes with human cells
2.correctFeedback: ✅ Right — stopping early means some bacteria (those with partial resistance) may survive. Without the continued selection pressure of the antibiotic, they multiply unchecked, and resistance spreads.
2.wrongFeedback: When you stop early, the most susceptible bacteria have already been killed — but any bacteria with slight resistance may survive. Without continued antibiotic pressure to eliminate them, these resistant bacteria multiply. Completing the full course maximises the chance of killing all bacteria before resistance can spread.

3.question: Why is Lamarck's theory of evolution incorrect?
options: Lamarck said organisms change too slowly — Darwin's theory is faster
Lamarck's theory requires large populations, but evolution can happen in small ones
Characteristics acquired during an organism's lifetime are not encoded in DNA and cannot be inherited by offspring
Lamarck only studied plants, so his theory doesn't apply to animals
3.correctFeedback: ✅ Correct — Lamarck proposed that acquired characteristics (like a stretched muscle or a longer neck) could be inherited. We now know this is impossible — only changes to DNA can be inherited, and using a muscle more doesn't change your DNA.
3.wrongFeedback: Lamarck's theory fails because acquired characteristics — things that change during your lifetime through use or experience — are not encoded in your DNA. Only genetic variation (encoded in DNA) can be passed to offspring. A bodybuilder's muscles don't make their children stronger.

commonMistakes: Saying organisms 'decide' or 'try' to evolve — natural selection is not goal-directed. A mutation either happens to be useful in the current environment or it doesn't. There is no intention.

Saying 'the antibiotic caused the mutation' — mutations occur randomly, before and after antibiotics. The antibiotic is the selection pressure that determines which bacteria survive, not the cause of the mutation.
Confusing individual evolution with population evolution — individuals don't evolve during their lifetime. Evolution happens at the population level over many generations.
Mixing up Darwin and Lamarck — Lamarck said acquired traits are inherited; Darwin said natural selection acts on existing genetic variation. Darwin is correct. Lamarck is not.
Saying all bacteria are killed by antibiotics — the whole point of antibiotic resistance is that some bacteria survive. If all were killed, resistance couldn't evolve.
examTips: For natural selection questions, write in numbered steps: (1) variation exists, (2) competition, (3) better-adapted survive and reproduce, (4) alleles passed to offspring, (5) allele frequency increases over generations. Marks are awarded for each step.

Always apply the mechanism to the specific example given — if the question is about peppered moths, use peppered moth language; if it's antibiotic resistance, talk about bacteria. Generic answers score less.

When comparing Darwin and Lamarck, focus on the mechanism of inheritance — Lamarck said acquired traits are inherited; Darwin said only genetic variation is inherited. That's the core scientific disagreement.

revisionCardBullets: Evolution = change in inherited characteristics of a population over many generations
Natural selection mechanism: Variation → Competition → Survival of the fittest → Alleles inherited → Frequency increases
Individuals do not evolve — populations evolve over generations
Antibiotic resistance: random mutation → resistant bacterium survives → reproduces → resistance spreads
Misuse of antibiotics (not completing courses) accelerates resistance
MRSA = classic superbug produced by natural selection
Evidence for evolution: fossil record, antibiotic resistance (direct observation), homologous structures, DNA comparison
Darwin: natural selection acts on existing genetic variation — correct theory
Lamarck: acquired characteristics inherited (wrong — not encoded in DNA)
Selection pressure = the environmental factor that determines which variants survive (e.g. antibiotics, predators)
